feedback.text: Greetings, For many years, users of digital goods have faced a persistent problem. They purchase something with real money but receive a product that may not be usable in the future. It would be worthwhile to find a way to address this issue. Of course, there are challenges such as expiring licenses (e.g., music in a video game) or the cost of maintaining servers. However, ideally, a given digital product should still be runnableat least without social featuresand servers should be easy for users themselves to host. Being able to run a digital good that one has paid for should not be a matter of debate. This is not about confirming or appropriating a licensesince, for obvious reasons, users do not own the license itselfbut about ensuring that the product we purchased remains available for our own personal use. Naturally, the systems on which these products run evolve or become obsolete, but if someone owns a functioning old computer or device, they should still be able to use the digital good. This highlights the problem with ownership of digital media. Many companies shut down digital goods, like games, for various reasons. But that doesnt change the fact that we bought the product and want to be able to use it at any time. It would be a different matter if the product were defective or infected with malware, but even then, it would be helpful to have the option to obtain a working copy at a later date. Perhaps it would be enough to introduce some kind of certificate for each digital copy? One that contains data such as the time of purchase, how much was paid, etc., and could be included with physical media as well. Then, even if we lost access to a digital good, we could confirm our purchase and recover what we rightfully ownpossibly through a special website. Such a certificate could also be stored like a purchase receipt, saved on the users drive. This is somewhat similar to what GOG does with video games. We buy a game, and it becomes our own. We cannot sell it, but we can store it and run it in the future. They even have a dedicated program: the GOG Preservation Program. To sum up, digital goods purchased by users should remain available to those users, even long after sales and support from developers and creators have ended. Digital goods have the unique advantage of not physically degrading and, in theory, could function indefinitely. But current practices can suddenly render our purchased product worthless. Best regards, Paul
